content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/justinbeaudry/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/5.1.0/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history — you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration

**Continuous execution:** Do not pause to check in with your human partner between tasks. Execute all tasks from the plan without stopping. The only reasons to stop are: BLOCKED status you cannot resolve, ambiguity that genuinely prevents progress, or all tasks complete. "Should I continue?" prompts and progress summaries waste their time — they asked you to execute the plan, so execute it.

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture, design, and review tasks**: use the most capable available model.

**Task complexity signals:**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec → cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns → standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding → most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Proceed to spec compliance review.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Prompt Templates

- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra

[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ❌ Issues:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)

[Implementer fixes issues]
Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting

[Spec reviewer reviews again]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant now

[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Implementer fixes]
Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Code reviewer reviews again]
Code reviewer: ✅ Approved

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is ✅** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - Ensures isolated workspace (creates one or verifies existing)
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution


ARGUMENTS: Execute docs/plans/2026-06-17-001-feat-hierarchy-discovery-plan.md (Plan 1/3, internal/hierarchy discovery package) on branch feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests. 6 tasks, all TDD.

I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute Plan 1. I'm already on `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests` (not main), and I have all 6 tasks' full text in context. Let me read the dispatch prompt templates first.

# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
  prompt: |
    You are implementing Task N: [task name]

    ## Task Description

    [FULL TEXT of task from plan - paste it here, don't make subagent read file]

    ## Context

    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]

    ## Before You Begin

    If you have questions about:
    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    - The approach or implementation strategy
    - Dependencies or assumptions
    - Anything unclear in the task description

    **Ask them now.** Raise any concerns before starting work.

    ## Your Job

    Once you're clear on requirements:
    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
    3. Verify implementation works
    4. Commit your work
    5. Self-review (see below)
    6. Report back

    Work from: [directory]

    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.

    ## Code Organization

    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS — don't split files on your own without plan guidance
    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
      and note it as a concern in your report
    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.

    ## When You're in Over Your Head

    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.

    **STOP and escalate when:**
    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress

    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
    or break the task into smaller pieces.

    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review

    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:

    **Completeness:**
    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    - Did I miss any requirements?
    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?

    **Quality:**
    - Is this my best work?
    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
    - Is the code clean and maintainable?

    **Discipline:**
    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
    - Did I only build what was requested?
    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?

    **Testing:**
    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
    - Did I follow TDD if required?
    - Are tests comprehensive?

    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.

    ## Report Format

    When done, report:
    - **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
    - What you implemented (or what you attempted, if blocked)
    - What you tested and test results
    - Files changed
    - Self-review findings (if any)
    - Any issues or concerns

    Use DONE_WITH_CONCERNS if you completed the work but have doubts about correctness.
    Use BLOCKED if you cannot complete the task. Use NEEDS_CONTEXT if you need
    information that wasn't provided. Never silently produce work you're unsure about.
```

input.description: Spec compliance review: hierarchy
input.prompt:
You are reviewing whether an implementation matches its specification. Be skeptical — verify by reading the actual code, not by trusting any report.

## Repo
/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`.

## What was requested
The specification is the plan file `docs/plans/2026-06-17-001-feat-hierarchy-discovery-plan.md`. Read it. It defines a pure `internal/hierarchy` package with exact code for:
- `hierarchy.go`: `Level` (LevelUser/LevelProject/LevelDirectory) + `Level.String()`, `Scope{Root, ManifestPath, Level, Emit}`, `Options{Home, IncludeUser, MaxHops}`.
- `discover.go`: `manifestAt`, `hasGit`, `findProjectRoot`, `collectEmitScopes`, `userScope`, `Discover`.
- Test files covering Level.String, manifest/git probes, project-root detection (incl. stops-at-home), emit-scope collection + ordering, user scope (read-only vs IncludeUser), and Discover edge cases (full hierarchy ordering user→project→directory, IncludeUser, no-git fallback = cwd-only, cwd-is-home no duplicate, no-manifests-empty).

## What the implementer claims
6 commits (dd8e853..2b7d6f4) implementing all of Tasks 1–6. Verification gate green: `go test -race -cover ./internal/hierarchy/...` → 87.3% coverage; vet+lint 0 issues; `go build ./...` ok. Two deviations claimed: a `//nolint:unparam` directive on `collectEmitScopes` (always-nil error return), and a gofmt whitespace normalization in a test.

## Your job — verify independently
1. Read `internal/hierarchy/hierarchy.go` and `internal/hierarchy/discover.go`. Confirm the types, fields, functions, and logic match the plan's code (especially: ordering is user→project→directory shallow→deep; no-git fallback yields only cwd's manifest classified as project; cwd==home is not double-counted; project-root detection stops at home).
2. Read `internal/hierarchy/hierarchy_test.go` and `internal/hierarchy/discover_test.go`. Confirm the tests present match the plan and actually assert behavior (not trivially passing).
3. Run `go test -race -cover ./internal/hierarchy/...` yourself and confirm it passes with the claimed coverage.
4. Check for MISSING requirements (any test or function from the plan absent) and EXTRA work (anything beyond the plan — extra files, fields, functions, features). The plan says this is a pure leaf importing only stdlib + `internal/workspace`; confirm no other imports or files were added, and nothing outside `internal/hierarchy/` was changed (use `git diff --stat fef517c..HEAD`).
5. Assess the two claimed deviations: is the `//nolint:unparam` acceptable given the plan-mandated signature, or should it be flagged? Is the gofmt change purely whitespace?

## Report
- ✅ Spec compliant, OR ❌ Issues found — list specifically what is missing or extra, with file:line references. Be concrete.

content.text:
Everything verified. The only internal dependency is `internal/workspace` (the package itself plus that), exactly as the plan mandates. `unparam` is indeed an enabled linter in `.golangci.yml`, which explains why the directive is needed.

## ✅ Spec compliant

I independently read both source files, both test files, the plan, and the workspace constants, then ran the full gate myself. The implementation matches the specification exactly.

**Types (`internal/hierarchy/hierarchy.go`)** — `Level` with `LevelUser`/`LevelProject`/`LevelDirectory` (iota order correct), `Level.String()` with the `unknown` default, `Scope{Root, ManifestPath, Level, Emit}`, and `Options{Home, IncludeUser, MaxHops}` all match the plan byte-for-byte including doc comments.

**Logic (`internal/hierarchy/discover.go`)** — verified against each plan requirement:
- `manifestAt` uses `os.Stat` + `IsRegular()` so a directory named like the manifest is rejected (discover.go:14-21).
- `hasGit` uses `os.Lstat` on `.git` (handles worktree file form) (discover.go:25-28).
- `findProjectRoot` walks up, returns `false` at `dir == home` *before* the git check, so project-root detection stops at home (discover.go:35-51).
- `collectEmitScopes` walks cwd→projectRoot, classifies projectRoot as `LevelProject` and others as `LevelDirectory`, then reverses to shallow→deep (discover.go:63-93).
- `Discover` ordering is user→project→directory; no-git fallback yields only cwd's own manifest classified `LevelProject` and is guarded by `absCwd != home` so cwd==home is not double-counted (discover.go:149-156).

**Tests** — all 16 functions from the plan are present and assert real behavior (counts, roots, levels, emit flags, ordering). `Level.String` covers all four cases; manifest/git probes, project-root (incl. stops-at-home), emit-scope ordering, user-scope read-only vs IncludeUser, and all 5 Discover edge cases are present. None are trivially passing.

**Gate (run fresh by me):**
- `go test -race -cover -count=1 ./internal/hierarchy/...` → PASS, **87.3%** coverage (matches claim).
- `go vet` → clean; `golangci-lint run` → **0 issues**; `go build ./...` → ok.

**Scope containment:** `git diff --stat fef517c..HEAD` shows only the 4 files in `internal/hierarchy/` (555 insertions, nothing deleted/modified elsewhere). `go list -deps` confirms the package's only internal import is `internal/workspace` — a pure leaf as specified. No extra files, fields, or functions.

**Two claimed deviations — both acceptable:**
1. `//nolint:unparam` on `collectEmitScopes` (discover.go:62): justified. The plan mandates the `([]Scope, error)` signature so `Discover` can propagate via `emit, err = collectEmitScopes(...)`, but the body never returns a non-nil error. `unparam` *is* enabled in `.golangci.yml` (lines 23, 48), so without the directive lint would fail. The signature is plan-required, the comment explains the intent (reserved for future I/O), and it's the narrowest possible suppression. Not a concern.
2. gofmt whitespace in `TestDiscoverNoGitFallbackIsCwdOnly`: confirmed purely comment-alignment. The plan source had `writeManifest(t, parent)   //` with extra padding spaces aligning two comments; gofmt collapsed them to single-space (discover_test.go:258-259). No logic, identifier, or assertion change.

No missing requirements, no extra work.

input.prompt:
You are doing a code quality review of a completed, spec-compliant change. Spec compliance has already passed — focus on whether the code is well-built (clean, correct, maintainable, well-tested), not on whether it matches the spec.

## Repo / diff
Repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`.
Review the diff from BASE `fef517c` to HEAD `2b7d6f4`:
  git diff fef517c..2b7d6f4 -- internal/hierarchy/
This adds a new pure leaf package `internal/hierarchy` (discovery of agent-sync manifests up the directory tree). Requirements source: `docs/plans/2026-06-17-001-feat-hierarchy-discovery-plan.md`.

## What to evaluate
Standard Go code-quality concerns plus:
- Does each file have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface? (`hierarchy.go` = types, `discover.go` = algorithm.)
- Are the units (`findProjectRoot`, `collectEmitScopes`, `userScope`, `Discover`) decomposed so they can be understood and tested independently?
- Correctness of the walk logic and edge cases: symlinked paths (note `filepath.Abs` does not resolve symlinks; `internal/workspace` deliberately uses logical paths — is that consistent here?), the `maxHops` bound, behavior when `cwd` is outside `home`, the cwd==home dedup guard, and ordering (user→project→directory).
- Test quality: do tests assert real behavior and cover the branches? Any gaps (e.g. MaxHops bound, the `Options.MaxHops` default path, symlink behavior) that matter?
- Naming, comments, error wrapping (`fmt.Errorf("%w")`), idioms.
- Did this change introduce files that are already too large, or any dead code / YAGNI violations? (The `//nolint:unparam` on `collectEmitScopes`'s always-nil error return is known and plan-mandated — you may comment on it but it is accepted.)

Read the actual code. Run `go test -race ./internal/hierarchy/...` if useful.

## Report
Return: Strengths; Issues grouped Critical / Important / Minor (each with file:line and a concrete fix); and an overall Assessment (Approved / Approved-with-minor / Changes-needed). Be rigorous but do not invent problems — if it's clean, say so.

content.text:
Clean lint. I have everything I need to write the review.

Let me do a final consideration of edge cases the prompt asked about:

- **Symlinks**: `Discover` uses `filepath.Abs` + `Clean`, no `EvalSymlinks`. This is intentionally consistent with `internal/workspace.absLogical`, which the package comment and plan both call out. A symlinked cwd is treated as its logical path; `.git`/manifest lookups happen against logical ancestors. This is the deliberate project convention — correct and consistent.
- **`maxHops` bound**: enforced in `findProjectRoot`. `collectEmitScopes` is unbounded but only runs on an already-bounded cwd→root distance, so safe. Worth a one-line comment but not a defect.
- **cwd outside home**: `findProjectRoot`'s `dir == home` check only triggers if the walk passes through home. If cwd is on a different branch than home (never reaching home), the walk terminates at filesystem root or a `.git` — correct.
- **cwd==home dedup**: guarded by `else if absCwd != home`, tested by `TestDiscoverCwdIsHomeNoDuplicate`. Correct.
- **Ordering**: user→project→directory, with directory scopes shallow→deep. Tested by `TestDiscoverFullHierarchy`. Correct.

Here's my review.

---

## Code Quality Review: `internal/hierarchy`

### Strengths

- **Clean separation of concerns.** `hierarchy.go` holds only types/docs (`Level`, `Scope`, `Options`); `discover.go` holds the algorithm. Each helper (`manifestAt`, `hasGit`, `findProjectRoot`, `collectEmitScopes`, `userScope`) does exactly one thing and is independently testable — and the tests exercise them at that granularity rather than only through `Discover`.
- **Excellent documentation.** The package comment precisely states the contract and the non-goals ("pure leaf package... performs no writes, opens no fsroot"). Every exported symbol and every helper has a doc comment explaining not just what but *why* (e.g. why home is never a project root, why the user scope is returned read-only).
- **Correct, consistent path handling.** `Discover` uses `filepath.Abs`+`Clean` without `EvalSymlinks`, which deliberately matches `internal/workspace.absLogical` (logical-path convention). This is the right call and is consistent across the two packages — the leaf package does not invent a different symlink policy.
- **Edge cases are genuinely handled, not just claimed.** The cwd==home dedup guard (`else if absCwd != home`), the "no `.git` ancestor → cwd-only project fallback", the home-as-project-root exclusion, and shallow→deep ordering are all implemented cleanly and covered by named tests asserting real behavior (roots, levels, and `Emit` flags — not just counts).
- **Idiomatic Go.** Error wrapping uses `%w` with contextual prefixes; the `manifestAt` `IsRegular()` check correctly rejects a *directory* named `.agent-sync.yaml` (tested). `go test -race`, `go vet`, and `golangci-lint run` are all clean. Coverage 87.3%.
- **The `//nolint:unparam` is justified and documented** (stable contract for `Discover` to propagate; reserved for future I/O). Accepted per the plan.

### Issues

**Critical** — none.

**Important** — none.

**Minor**

1. **Test gap: `MaxHops` is never exercised with a non-default value.** `discover.go:35` (`findProjectRoot`) and `discover.go:138` (`Discover`'s `maxHops <= 0` default). Every call site in the tests passes `workspace.DefaultMaxHops` or leaves `Options.MaxHops` zero, so the hop-budget exhaustion branch (`findProjectRoot`'s trailing `return "", false`) and the `Options.MaxHops` override path through `Discover` are untested. I verified empirically that the bound *works* (a scratch test with a `.git` 3 hops up and a budget of 2 correctly returns `ok=false`), so this is a coverage gap, not a defect. Fix: add `TestFindProjectRootMaxHopsExhausted` (deep `.git`, small budget → not found) and a `Discover` case with `Options{MaxHops: 1}` to lock the bound against regression.

2. **`collectEmitScopes` has an unbounded loop while `findProjectRoot` is bounded** (`discover.go:66`). It's safe in practice because `collectEmitScopes` only runs after `findProjectRoot` located a root within `maxHops` of cwd, so the cwd→root distance is already bounded, and the `parent == dir` filesystem-root guard is a backstop. The asymmetry is slightly surprising though. Consider a one-line comment at the loop noting that the distance is pre-bounded by the `findProjectRoot` precondition (the doc comment states `projectRoot must be cwd or an ancestor` but doesn't connect it to the missing hop cap).

3. **`TestDiscoverNoGitFallbackIsCwdOnly` has dead scaffolding** (`discover_test.go:255-260`): `notes` and `parent` are assigned the same value (`filepath.Join(home, "notes")`), and the `_ = parent` plus `_ = parent`-style noise (`parent` is used only by `writeManifest`) make the setup harder to read than it needs to be. The test asserts the right thing, but the duplicate variable and the `_ = parent` blank-assign are clutter. Minor cleanup: drop `parent`, write the manifest to `notes` directly.

### Assessment

**Approved-with-minor.** The code is well-structured, correct across the edge cases called out (symlinks/logical paths, the cwd==home dedup, home-not-a-root, no-`.git` fallback, and ordering), idiomatic, and well-documented; the full gate (`go test -race`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`) is green at 87.3% coverage. The only substantive item is the untested `MaxHops` bound (Minor #1) — worth adding a regression test before this becomes a foundation other code depends on, but it functions correctly today. Issues #2 and #3 are polish.

Relevant files:
- `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/hierarchy/discover.go`
- `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/hierarchy/hierarchy.go`
- `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/hierarchy/discover_test.go`

input.prompt:

## A. Report package (`internal/report`)
I need to aggregate multiple `engine.Sync` results (one per scope) and render them. Report verbatim:
1. `report.Summary` struct definition (all fields).
2. `report.TargetReport` struct + `report.Counts` + the status constants (StatusOK/Unchanged/Blocked/Failed/Skipped).
3. `report.Outcome` (or whatever carries `ExitCode`) — how ExitCode is computed.
4. `func Summarize(...)` signature.
5. `func RenderText(Summary) string` and `func MarshalJSON(Summary)` signatures.
6. Anything like `report.Mode` (ModeAtomic/ModeBestEffort).
List the file:line for each.

## B. Adapter package (`internal/adapter`)
For a "coverage analyzer" that asks each adapter whether a given node kind is read natively at a given hierarchy level, I need to know how adapter capabilities are currently declared. Report verbatim:
1. `adapter.Registry` type + its methods (esp. `Get`, and any list/iterate/`All`/`Names` method).
2. `adapter.BundledAdapter` type + how a bundled adapter is constructed (the `Bundled()` funcs). What interface/methods does a bundled adapter expose to the engine?

Read `internal/adapter/bundled/claude/capabilities.go` and `internal/adapter/bundled/claude/bundled.go` and quote the capability/DeclaredOutputs declaration. Where do these come from at runtime — the wire `InitializeResult.Capabilities`/`DeclaredOutputs` (`pkg/adapterkit/types.go`), or a static Go declaration on the bundled adapter?
4. The `contract`/`adapterkit` capability types: `Capabilities`, `DeclaredOutput`, `OutputMode` constants, and the IR `Kind` values an adapter handles.
5. Is there any existing per-adapter static metadata accessible WITHOUT spinning up a session (i.e., can I get an adapter's name + reserved prefix + declared outputs synchronously from the registry/BundledAdapter, or must I Initialize a session)?

## C. cmd_status + cmd_validate wiring

7. Confirm `engine.Sync` and `engine.Plan` both take a single `engine.Request` with one `Root` (already known) — note any other entry points.

Return concrete verbatim signatures and file:line refs, organized by section. Quote actual code, not paraphrase.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive report with exact file:line references.

## Summary of API Findings

### A. Report Package (`internal/report`)

**1. `report.Summary` struct definition** (lines 69-77 in `/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync/internal/report/summary.go`):
```go
type Summary struct {
	Workspace   string         `json:"workspace"`
	Commit      string         `json:"commit"`
	GeneratedAt string         `json:"generated_at"`
	Mode        Mode           `json:"mode"`
	Targets     []TargetReport `json:"targets"`
	Outcome     Outcome        `json:"summary"`

You are implementing an integration feature in the agent-sync Go project at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, on branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests` (already checked out — do NOT create/switch branches).

## Your task
Execute the plan at `docs/plans/2026-06-17-002-feat-multi-scope-sync-plan.md` (Tasks 1–5), in order. Read it fully first — it specifies the orchestration design, exact new types/signatures, test scenarios, and the existing files you must integrate with.

This is integration work, so unlike a pure-logic plan, you must read and follow the EXISTING code patterns:
- READ `internal/cli/setup.go` (prepareEngine/prepared), `internal/cli/cmd_sync.go` (the sync RunE + renderSummary), `internal/cli/materialize.go`, and especially the test harnesses in `internal/cli/cmd_sync_localdir_test.go` and `internal/cli/cmd_sync_test.go` — mirror how those tests construct a temp workspace, write `.agent-sync.yaml`, build the runtime context, and drive the command. Do NOT invent a new test harness; reuse the existing helpers.
- The `internal/hierarchy` package (already built) provides `Discover(cwd, hierarchy.Options{Home, IncludeUser, MaxHops}) ([]Scope, error)` and `Scope{Root, ManifestPath, Level, Emit}`.

## Hard requirements
- Reuse `engine.Sync` unchanged — do NOT modify `internal/engine`. The orchestration is a CLI-layer loop.
- Per-scope: build a `Request` via the extracted `prepareScope`, run `engine.Sync`, close the root, collect a `scopeOutcome`. Continue-and-report: a scope's prepare/sync error goes in `scopeOutcome.Err` and the loop continues; discovery failure aborts the whole run.
- `--user` flag → `IncludeUser`. A repo `sync` (no `--user`) must NEVER write under the home directory — assert this in a test.
- When the global `--workspace` override is set, keep today's single-scope behavior; `--user` + `--workspace` together is an error.
- Tests must be hermetic: inject the home directory (via `hierarchy.Options.Home` and/or a swappable `resolveHome` seam) so the suite never touches the real `~`. Follow the plan's test-seam guidance.

## Verification gate (must be green before you report DONE)
- `go test -race -cover ./internal/cli/... ./internal/hierarchy/...`
- `go vet ./... && golangci-lint run`
- `go build ./... && go test ./...`
Fix anything these surface (the repo enables errcheck/govet/staticcheck/unused/gosimple/ineffassign/unparam). Make the per-task commits with the exact messages in the plan.

## If blocked
If the existing test harness or runtimeContext construction is unclear, or an interface in the plan doesn't fit the real code, adapt to the real code (the plan's integration code is intent-level, not byte-exact) and report exactly what you changed and why. If you hit a genuine architectural ambiguity you can't resolve, STOP and report BLOCKED with specifics rather than guessing.

## Report back
Status (DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT), the final gate output (race/cover, vet, lint, build, full `go test ./...`), the commits made (SHAs + messages), files changed, any deviations from the plan with justification, and any concerns.

content.text:
Working tree clean, all work committed. 

## Status: DONE

Executed all five tasks of `docs/plans/2026-06-17-002-feat-multi-scope-sync-plan.md` on `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`.

### Verification gate (all green)
- `go test -race -cover ./internal/cli/... ./internal/hierarchy/...` → both pass; coverage cli 69.5%, hierarchy 88.7%
- `go vet ./...` → clean
- `golangci-lint run` → 0 issues
- `go build ./...` → clean
- `go test ./...` → all 32 packages pass
- Manual smoke: both project + directory scopes emit to their own `.claude`, nothing written under home, second run reports `unchanged` (idempotent)

### Commits
- `f9a0f6e` refactor(cli): extract prepareScope from prepareEngine
- `7d8109a` feat(cli): add multi-scope hierarchy sync orchestrator
- `9f01fa8` feat(cli): sync emits the manifest hierarchy; add --user

### Files changed
- `internal/cli/setup.go` — extracted `prepareScope(ctx, rc, scopeRoot, manifestPath, now)`; `prepareEngine` now resolves the workspace and delegates. `prepareScope` sets `prepared.Workspace` to a minimal `&workspace.Workspace{Root, ManifestPath}`.
- `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go` (new) — `scopeOutcome`, `hierarchySyncOptions`, `runHierarchySync` (continue-and-report; discovery failure aborts), `hierarchyExitCode`, `renderHierarchyText`, `renderHierarchyJSON`/`hierarchyScopeJSON`, `resolveHome` seam + `homeDir`, `errUserWithWorkspace`.
- `internal/cli/cmd_sync.go` — RunE branches: explicit `--workspace` keeps the single-scope path (extracted to `runSingleScopeSync`), default uses the hierarchy orchestrator; added `--user`; `--user`+`--workspace` is a hard error; `handleHierarchyPostMerge` applies post-merge yield per scope.
- `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go` (new) — orchestrator emit test (asserts no write under home), exit-code, text/JSON render, and command-level hierarchy/`--user`/mutual-exclusion tests.

### Deviations from the plan (all justified)
1. **Tasks 2 and 3 landed in two commits but the rendering/exit-code code was written into `hierarchy_sync.go` alongside the orchestrator** rather than as a strictly separate second diff. The two functions are inseparable in one file; the plan's two commit messages are both present and the test ordering (Task 2 emit test, then exit-code/render tests) is preserved. No behavior difference.
2. **Test home seam**: used the plan's preferred `var resolveHome = homeDir` swappable seam (not the `AGENT_SYNC_WORKSPACE_STOP_AT` env mechanism) for command-level tests, plus `hierarchy.Options.Home` injection for the direct-orchestrator test. The suite never touches the real `~`. Command tests use Go 1.25's `t.Chdir` to set cwd (auto-restored); none of these tests use `t.Parallel`, consistent with the existing suite.
3. **Post-merge for the hierarchy path**: since `runHierarchySync` closes each scope's root, `handleHierarchyPostMerge` re-opens the blocked scope's root via `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot` to write that scope's marker. Single-scope post-merge behavior is unchanged.

### Concerns
- None blocking. The `--user` + post-merge combination evaluates post-merge per scope (acceptable per plan §Task 4 step 3). `validate`/`status` remain single-scope as scoped; `status` hierarchy-awareness is deferred to Plan 3.

You are a skeptical spec-compliance reviewer. Verify by reading the actual code, not by trusting any report. Repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`.

## Spec
`docs/plans/2026-06-17-002-feat-multi-scope-sync-plan.md` (multi-scope sync orchestration + `--user`). Also the design `docs/brainstorms/2026-06-17-hierarchy-aware-manifests-design.md` §Components 5, §Data Flow, §Error Handling.

## Diff to review
`git diff 7607aa9..9f01fa8` — changes in `internal/cli/` (setup.go, cmd_sync.go, new hierarchy_sync.go, new hierarchy_sync_test.go).

## Verify independently
1. **prepareScope extraction** (setup.go): is `prepareEngine` behavior-preserving (still uses `workspace.Find` then delegates)? Does `prepareScope` build the Request from an explicit (root, manifestPath) and open an fsroot at that root?
2. **Orchestrator** (hierarchy_sync.go): does `runHierarchySync` use `hierarchy.Discover`, skip non-Emit scopes, run `engine.Sync` per emit scope, close each root, and implement continue-and-report (per-scope prepare/sync error recorded, loop continues; discovery error aborts the run)?
3. **`--user`**: does the flag map to `IncludeUser`? Critically — verify by reading the test that a plain `sync` (no `--user`) does NOT write under the home dir, and `sync --user` DOES emit the user scope. Confirm `--user` + `--workspace` is a hard error.
4. **`--workspace` back-compat**: when an explicit workspace override is set, is today's single-scope path preserved unchanged?
5. **engine untouched**: confirm `git diff 7607aa9..9f01fa8 -- internal/engine/` is empty (the orchestration must not modify the engine).
6. **Tests are real & hermetic**: do the tests assert emitted files on disk (not mocks)? Do they avoid touching the real `~` (via the `resolveHome` seam / `hierarchy.Options.Home`)? Run `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` yourself and confirm pass.
7. **Missing/extra**: anything in the plan not implemented, or extra features beyond the plan? Confirm `validate`/`status` were NOT changed (still single-scope; status hierarchy view is Plan 3) — `git diff 7607aa9..9f01fa8 -- internal/cli/cmd_status.go internal/cli/cmd_validate.go` should be empty.

## Report
✅ Spec compliant, OR ❌ Issues found — specific, with file:line. Note any deviations and whether they're acceptable.

content.text:
All checks complete. Here is my independent verdict.

## ✅ Spec compliant

I verified every claim by reading the actual code and running the suite — no report was trusted.

**1. prepareScope extraction (setup.go)** — PASS
- `prepareEngine` (setup.go:52) is behavior-preserving: nil-check → `workspace.Find(flags.Workspace, ...)` → delegates to `prepareScope(ctx, rc, ws.Root, ws.ManifestPath, now)`.
- `prepareScope` (setup.go:72) builds the Request from explicit `(scopeRoot, manifestPath)`: `manifest.LoadFile(manifestPath)`, `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot(scopeRoot)`, `materialize(...Root: root)`, `adapter.DiscoverAdapters`, and assembles `engine.Request{Root, WorkspacePath: scopeRoot, ...}`. Closes the root on error paths; returns a `Close` func for the success path.

**2. Orchestrator (hierarchy_sync.go:42)** — PASS
- Uses `hierarchy.Discover`; discovery error returns `nil, fmt.Errorf("discover hierarchy: %w", err)` — aborts the run.
- Skips `!sc.Emit` scopes (hierarchy_sync.go:50).
- Per emit scope: `prepareScope` → `engine.Sync` → `prep.Close()` (root closed every iteration, line 63). Prepare error or sync error is recorded in `out.Err`, appended, and the loop continues — genuine continue-and-report.

**3. `--user` (cmd_sync.go + tests)** — PASS
- Flag wired at cmd_sync.go:113, mapped to `hierarchySyncOptions{IncludeUser: userScope}` (line 80).
- `TestSyncCommandUserFlag` (test:142) is a real on-disk assertion: plain `sync` → `mustNotExist(home/.claude)` (line 154); `sync --user` → `mustExist(home/.claude/skills/agent-sync-user-skill/SKILL.md)` (line 160).
- `--user` + `--workspace` is a hard error: `errUserWithWorkspace` returned at cmd_sync.go:65 *before* the single-scope path; `TestSyncCommandUserWithWorkspaceIsError` (test:163) confirms a non-nil error.

**4. `--workspace` back-compat** — PASS. When `rc.Flags.Workspace != ""`, `runSingleScopeSync` (cmd_sync.go:122) runs today's path verbatim: `prepareEngine` → `engine.Sync` → `anyBlocked`/post-merge → `renderSummary` → `exitError`. Hierarchy discovery is skipped.

**5. engine untouched** — PASS. `git diff 7607aa9..9f01fa8 -- internal/engine/` is empty.

**6. Tests real & hermetic** — PASS. Tests assert emitted files on disk (`mustExist`/`mustNotExist`), no mocks. Home is `t.TempDir()`, injected via `hierarchy.Options.Home` and the swappable `resolveHome` seam (test:117-119, restored via `t.Cleanup`). The real `~` is never touched. `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` → `ok` (5.5s). `go vet` clean; `golangci-lint run ./internal/cli/...` → 0 issues; `go build ./...` exit 0.

**7. Missing/extra** — PASS. `cmd_status.go` and `cmd_validate.go` diffs are empty — both stay single-scope, status hierarchy view correctly deferred to Plan 3. No scope creep.

**Deviations (all acceptable):**
- `renderHierarchyJSON` marshals `report.Summary` directly via `json.Marshal` rather than `report.MarshalJSON`. Explicitly permitted by the plan (line 299); `Summary` carries full JSON tags so the wire shape matches.
- The aggregate JSON document adds `schema_version: 1` — a sensible, plan-consistent extra (the plan defined the per-scope `hierarchyScopeJSON` shape and left the envelope to the implementer). Tests cover it.

No issues found. The diff implements design §Components 5, §Data Flow, and §Error Handling (continue-and-report; discovery-abort; `--user`-gated home writes) and the `--workspace` back-compat path as specified.

- `prepareEngine` (setup.go:52) is behavior-preserving: nil-check → `workspace.Find(flags.Workspace, ...)` → delegates to `prepareScope(ctx, rc, ws.Root, ws.ManifestPath, now)`.
- `prepareScope` (setup.go:72) builds the Request from explicit `(scopeRoot, manifestPath)`: `manifest.LoadFile(manifestPath)`, `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot(scopeRoot)`, `materialize(...Root: root)`, `adapter.DiscoverAdapters`, and assembles `engine.Request{Root, WorkspacePath: scopeRoot, ...}`. Closes the root on error paths; returns a `Close` func for the success path.

**2. Orchestrator (hierarchy_sync.go:42)** — PASS
- Uses `hierarchy.Discover`; discovery error returns `nil, fmt.Errorf("discover hierarchy: %w", err)` — aborts the run.
- Skips `!sc.Emit` scopes (hierarchy_sync.go:50).
- Per emit scope: `prepareScope` → `engine.Sync` → `prep.Close()` (root closed every iteration, line 63). Prepare error or sync error is recorded in `out.Err`, appended, and the loop continues — genuine continue-and-report.

**3. `--user` (cmd_sync.go + tests)** — PASS
- Flag wired at cmd_sync.go:113, mapped to `hierarchySyncOptions{IncludeUser: userScope}` (line 80).
- `TestSyncCommandUserFlag` (test:142) is a real on-disk assertion: plain `sync` → `mustNotExist(home/.claude)` (line 154); `sync --user` → `mustExist(home/.claude/skills/agent-sync-user-skill/SKILL.md)` (line 160).
- `--user` + `--workspace` is a hard error: `errUserWithWorkspace` returned at cmd_sync.go:65 *before* the single-scope path; `TestSyncCommandUserWithWorkspaceIsError` (test:163) confirms a non-nil error.

**4. `--workspace` back-compat** — PASS. When `rc.Flags.Workspace != ""`, `runSingleScopeSync` (cmd_sync.go:122) runs today's path verbatim: `prepareEngine` → `engine.Sync` → `anyBlocked`/post-merge → `renderSummary` → `exitError`. Hierarchy discovery is skipped.

**5. engine untouched** — PASS. `git diff 7607aa9..9f01fa8 -- internal/engine/` is empty.

**6. Tests real & hermetic** — PASS. Tests assert emitted files on disk (`mustExist`/`mustNotExist`), no mocks. Home is `t.TempDir()`, injected via `hierarchy.Options.Home` and the swappable `resolveHome` seam (test:117-119, restored via `t.Cleanup`). The real `~` is never touched. `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` → `ok` (5.5s). `go vet` clean; `golangci-lint run ./internal/cli/...` → 0 issues; `go build ./...` exit 0.

**7. Missing/extra** — PASS. `cmd_status.go` and `cmd_validate.go` diffs are empty — both stay single-scope, status hierarchy view correctly deferred to Plan 3. No scope creep.

**Deviations (all acceptable):**
- `renderHierarchyJSON` marshals `report.Summary` directly via `json.Marshal` rather than `report.MarshalJSON`. Explicitly permitted by the plan (line 299); `Summary` carries full JSON tags so the wire shape matches.
- The aggregate JSON document adds `schema_version: 1` — a sensible, plan-consistent extra (the plan defined the per-scope `hierarchyScopeJSON` shape and left the envelope to the implementer). Tests cover it.

No issues found. The diff implements design §Components 5, §Data Flow, and §Error Handling (continue-and-report; discovery-abort; `--user`-gated home writes) and the `--workspace` back-compat path as specified.

Spec review: **✅ Spec compliant** — independently verified, engine untouched, status/validate unchanged, `--user` home-write gating asserted on disk, tests hermetic. Two acceptable deviations (direct `json.Marshal`, a `schema_version` envelope field). Now code quality review.

input.description: Quality review: multi-scope sync
input.prompt:
Code quality review of a completed, spec-compliant change (spec already passed — focus on whether it's well-built). Repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`.

## Diff
`git diff 7607aa9..9f01fa8 -- internal/cli/` — adds multi-scope `sync` orchestration: `prepareScope` extraction (setup.go), new `hierarchy_sync.go` (orchestrator, rendering, exit code, home seam), `cmd_sync.go` wiring + `--user`, and `hierarchy_sync_test.go`. Requirements: `docs/plans/2026-06-17-002-feat-multi-scope-sync-plan.md`.

## Evaluate
- Clean separation: is `hierarchy_sync.go` cohesive (orchestration only)? Is `cmd_sync.go` still a thin command def? Did `prepareScope`/`prepareEngine` stay DRY?
- Correctness/robustness of the orchestration loop: every scope's root is reliably Closed even on error paths (no leaked `os.Root` handles); continue-and-report doesn't swallow errors silently (errors surface in output + exit code); the post-merge re-open-root logic is sound.
- Error handling & wrapping (`%w`), naming, comments, idioms. Is the `resolveHome` seam clean (not leaking test-only concerns into prod in an ugly way)?
- Rendering: text + JSON aggregate — any correctness issues (e.g. nil summary on error scopes, exit-code aggregation across scopes)?
- Test quality: do tests cover the important branches (multi-scope emit, --user on/off, --user+--workspace error, continue-and-report with a failing scope, JSON output)? Is there a test for a scope that FAILS while others succeed (continue-and-report)? If that's missing, flag it.
- Did this change grow any file unreasonably, or introduce dead code / YAGNI?

Read the code; run `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` if useful.

## Report
Strengths; Issues grouped Critical / Important / Minor (file:line + concrete fix); overall Assessment (Approved / Approved-with-minor / Changes-needed). Don't invent problems.

content.text:
I have a complete picture. `Level` has a `String()` method, so `%s` in text and `.String()` in JSON produce the same value — that's fine, just a stylistic inconsistency. Let me verify the post-merge re-open-root logic one more time and check whether the single-scope path's `--user` rejection happens before or after work (it's correctly first). Let me confirm `OpenWorkspaceRoot` for the post-merge re-open is the right call given the original root is already closed.

The orchestrator closes each `prep.Root` inside the loop (`prep.Close()`), so by the time `handleHierarchyPostMerge` runs, the roots are closed — hence the re-open via `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot`. That's sound. Let me verify the resource-handling on the prepare-error path once more and check for the missing continue-and-report test.

The analysis is complete. Let me write up the review.

## Code Quality Review: Multi-Scope Sync Orchestration

**Branch:** `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests` | **Range:** `7607aa9..9f01fa8`
**Verification:** `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` PASS (5.0s), `golangci-lint run` 0 issues.

### Strengths

- **Clean separation as planned.** `hierarchy_sync.go` is cohesive orchestration-only; `cmd_sync.go` stays a thin command def with flag wiring + branching; `prepareScope`/`prepareEngine` extraction is textbook DRY (`prepareEngine` = `workspace.Find` + delegate). All three match the plan's stated structure exactly.
- **Resource handling is correct on every path.** In `runHierarchySync` the prepare-error path appends and `continue`s without ever opening a root (the leak window doesn't exist — `prepareScope` itself closes its root on internal failure, returning no handle). The success path calls `prep.Close()` unconditionally after `engine.Sync`, before inspecting `serr`, so the `os.Root` handle is released even when sync errors. No leaked handles.
- **Continue-and-report is real, not swallowed.** A failing scope's error lands in `scopeOutcome.Err`, surfaces in both renderers (`ERROR: %v` / JSON `error` field) and forces exit 1 via `hierarchyExitCode`. Discovery failure correctly aborts the whole run (indeterminate scope set) — the right asymmetry.
- **`resolveHome` seam is clean.** A package-level `var resolveHome = homeDir` swapped via `t.Cleanup`; production never sees test concerns, and the doc comment explains why. This is the idiomatic Go test seam, not an ugly injection.
- **Post-merge re-open logic is sound.** Because the loop closes each `prep.Root` eagerly, `handleHierarchyPostMerge` correctly re-opens via `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot` per blocked scope, writes the marker, and closes again. Warn-and-continue on re-open failure is the right call for a hook-mode best-effort path.
- **Error wrapping** uses `%w` throughout (`discover hierarchy: %w`, `sync: %w`, etc.); sentinel `errUserWithWorkspace` is a proper `errors.New` var with a clear message; the `--user`+`--workspace` rejection happens *before* any work.

### Issues

**Critical:** None.

**Important:**

1. **Missing test: a scope that FAILS while others SUCCEED (the headline continue-and-report behavior).** This is the one real gap. `TestHierarchyExitCode`, `TestRenderHierarchyText`, and `TestRenderHierarchyJSON` all exercise mixed outcomes using *hand-built* `scopeOutcome` slices — they test the renderers/exit-code in isolation, not the orchestrator's loop. No test drives `runHierarchySync` (or the `sync` command) through a tree where one scope's `prepareScope`/`engine.Sync` actually fails and asserts that (a) the other scope still emitted its files and (b) outcomes carries one `Err` and one `Summary`. The loop's `continue`-after-error branch (`hierarchy_sync.go:55-59`) is currently uncovered end-to-end. Add a test with, e.g., a malformed `.agent-sync.yaml` in the nested scope and a valid one at the project root; assert the project scope's `.claude` tree exists, `outcomes[nested].Err != nil`, and the run exits non-zero. (`internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go`)

**Minor:**

2. **Aggregate JSON embeds a different summary shape than `--output=json`.** `renderHierarchyJSON` (`hierarchy_sync.go:125-155`) marshals `*report.Summary` via std-lib `json.Marshal`, yielding the raw struct tags (`workspace`, `commit`, `generated_at`, `mode`, `targets`, `summary`) with **no `schema_version`** — whereas the single-scope path's `report.MarshalJSON` wraps each summary in a versioned `jsonDocument` and normalizes `nil` targets to `[]`. Consequences: (a) a per-scope summary inside the aggregate has no `schema_version` (the wrapper has its own top-level one, version 1, so it's defensible but inconsistent); (b) the empty-targets-as-`[]`-not-`null` guarantee that `report.MarshalJSON` enforces is lost for the embedded summaries unless `Summarize` already populated them (it does for real runs, so this is latent, not active). If JSON consumers are expected to parse per-scope entries with the same parser as the standalone document, they'll diverge. Worth a one-line comment documenting the intentional shape difference, or reusing `report.MarshalJSON` output as a `json.RawMessage` per scope. (`hierarchy_sync.go:135-137`)

3. **Text renderer prints raw wrapped errors inline.** `renderHierarchyText` does `fmt.Fprintf(w, "ERROR: %v\n", o.Err)` (`hierarchy_sync.go:101`). A `prepareScope` failure produces a wrapped chain like `load manifest: ...: ...` which can be multi-line/verbose, rendered without indentation under the `== level: root ==` header. Cosmetic only; the existing `TestRenderHierarchyText` confirms the substring appears. No fix required — flag for awareness.

4. **Stylistic: `Level` rendered two ways.** Text uses `%s` (Stringer) at `hierarchy_sync.go:97`; JSON uses `o.Scope.Level.String()` at line 130. Both yield identical output since `Level` implements `Stringer`, so this is harmless — just pick one for consistency.

### Assessment

**Approved-with-minor.** The orchestration is well-built: correct resource lifecycle, genuine continue-and-report, clean layering, hermetic test seam, and it passes `-race` + lint cleanly. No file grew unreasonably (`hierarchy_sync.go` is 175 focused lines) and there's no dead code or YAGNI. The only thing I'd ask for before merge is **Issue #1** — the marquee feature (one scope fails, others still emit) is asserted only via synthetic `scopeOutcome` fixtures, never through the actual orchestration loop. Issues #2–#4 are documentation/polish and can ride along or follow up.

input.description: Fix Plan 2 review: add e2e test
input.prompt:
Two small code-review follow-ups in /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync (branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`, already checked out). Touch only `internal/cli/`.

## Fix 1 (Important) — End-to-end continue-and-report test
The orchestrator `runHierarchySync` in `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go` implements continue-and-report (one scope's prepare/sync failure is recorded in `scopeOutcome.Err`, the loop continues, other scopes still emit). This is currently only tested with hand-built `scopeOutcome` fixtures, NOT end-to-end through the loop. Add a real test in `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go` that:
- Builds a temp hierarchy (mirror the existing tests in that file — same helpers, same `resolveHome`/`hierarchy.Options.Home` injection, same `t.Chdir`/command harness): a valid project-level manifest at the git-root scope, and a nested directory-level scope whose `.agent-sync.yaml` is MALFORMED (e.g. invalid YAML or a missing/invalid canonical source) so that scope's `prepareScope` (or `engine.Sync`) fails.
- Calls `runHierarchySync` directly (preferred, for a precise assertion on the returned `[]scopeOutcome`) AND/OR drives the `sync` command.
- Asserts: (a) the VALID scope still emitted its `.claude` files on disk; (b) the returned outcomes contain exactly one entry with `Err != nil` (the malformed scope) and one with `Err == nil` and a populated `Summary`; (c) `hierarchyExitCode(outcomes)` is non-zero.
Read the existing tests in `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go` and reuse their setup helpers — do not invent a new harness. Confirm WHICH layer fails (prepare vs sync) and assert accordingly; if the malformed manifest fails at a different layer than you expected, adapt the test to the real behavior and keep the continue-and-report assertions.

## Fix 2 (Minor) — Document the aggregate JSON shape
In `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go`, the aggregate JSON path marshals each `report.Summary` directly via `json.Marshal` rather than `report.MarshalJSON` (so embedded per-scope summaries carry no `schema_version` and rely on `Summarize` to populate `targets`). Add a short comment at that marshal site explaining this is intentional: the aggregate document carries its own top-level `schema_version`, and per-scope summaries are embedded raw (their JSON tags match the standalone shape minus the version wrapper). Do NOT change the output shape — comment only.

## Gate (must be green)
- `go test -race ./internal/cli/...`
- `go vet ./... && golangci-lint run`
- `go build ./...`

Commit: `git add internal/cli/ && git commit -m "test(cli): cover end-to-end continue-and-report; document aggregate JSON shape"`

## Report
Status (DONE/BLOCKED), which layer the malformed scope failed at (prepare or sync), the gate output, the new commit SHA, and confirm the new test asserts the valid scope still emitted while the bad scope reported an error.

content.text:
Status: **DONE**

**Failure layer:** The malformed scope fails at the **prepare** layer, not sync. `hierarchy.Discover` keys off manifest *presence* (`manifestAt`), not validity, so the corrupt scope is still discovered and entered into the orchestrator loop. It then fails inside `prepareScope` at `manifest.LoadFile`, before any `engine.Sync` call. The test documents this in a comment and asserts accordingly.

**New test (`TestRunHierarchySyncContinuesPastMalformedScope`)** drives `runHierarchySync` directly and confirms continue-and-report:
- (a) The **valid** project scope still emitted `repo/.claude/skills/agent-sync-proj-skill/SKILL.md` despite the sibling failure; the malformed scope emitted no `.claude`.
- (b) Exactly one outcome has `Err != nil` (the malformed directory scope); the valid one has `Err == nil` with a populated `Summary` (asserted via non-empty `Summary.Workspace`).
- (c) `hierarchyExitCode(outcomes)` is non-zero.
- It also asserts `runHierarchySync` returns no top-level error (discovery succeeded since both manifests are present).

It reuses the existing harness exactly: `hierarchyTree`, `newTestRuntime`, `mustExist`/`mustNotExist`, and the same `resolveHome`/`hierarchy.Options.Home` injection pattern (here via the direct `home` argument, mirroring `TestRunHierarchySyncEmitsEachScope`).

**Fix 2:** Added an explanatory comment in `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go` at the `entry.Summary = &s` site in `renderHierarchyJSON`, documenting that per-scope summaries are intentionally embedded raw via `json.Marshal(doc)` rather than `report.MarshalJSON` — the aggregate carries its own top-level `schema_version`, embedded summaries match the standalone shape minus the version wrapper, and `targets` is populated by `Summarize`. Verified against `internal/report/json.go` and `summary.go` that the claim is accurate. Output shape unchanged (comment-only).

**Gate (all green):**
- `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` → `ok ... 5.243s`
- `go vet ./...` → clean; `golangci-lint run` → `0 issues.`
- `go build ./...` → clean

**Commit SHA:** `9b503257a0436d5a295e2734a11ea4213430efd9` on `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests` (2 files changed, 85 insertions).

Add a short comment at that marshal site explaining this is intentional: the aggregate document carries its own top-level `schema_version`, and per-scope summaries are embedded raw (their JSON tags match the standalone shape minus the version wrapper). Do NOT change the output shape — comment only.

## Gate (must be green)
- `go test -race ./internal/cli/...`
- `go vet ./... && golangci-lint run`
- `go build ./...`

Commit: `git add internal/cli/ && git commit -m "test(cli): cover end-to-end continue-and-report; document aggregate JSON shape"`

## Report
Status (DONE/BLOCKED), which layer the malformed scope failed at (prepare or sync), the gate output, the new commit SHA, and confirm the new test asserts the valid scope still emitted while the bad scope reported an error.

content.text:
Status: **DONE**

**Failure layer:** The malformed scope fails at the **prepare** layer, not sync. `hierarchy.Discover` keys off manifest *presence* (`manifestAt`), not validity, so the corrupt scope is still discovered and entered into the orchestrator loop. It then fails inside `prepareScope` at `manifest.LoadFile`, before any `engine.Sync` call. The test documents this in a comment and asserts accordingly.

**New test (`TestRunHierarchySyncContinuesPastMalformedScope`)** drives `runHierarchySync` directly and confirms continue-and-report:
- (a) The **valid** project scope still emitted `repo/.claude/skills/agent-sync-proj-skill/SKILL.md` despite the sibling failure; the malformed scope emitted no `.claude`.
- (b) Exactly one outcome has `Err != nil` (the malformed directory scope); the valid one has `Err == nil` with a populated `Summary` (asserted via non-empty `Summary.Workspace`).
- (c) `hierarchyExitCode(outcomes)` is non-zero.
- It also asserts `runHierarchySync` returns no top-level error (discovery succeeded since both manifests are present).

It reuses the existing harness exactly: `hierarchyTree`, `newTestRuntime`, `mustExist`/`mustNotExist`, and the same `resolveHome`/`hierarchy.Options.Home` injection pattern (here via the direct `home` argument, mirroring `TestRunHierarchySyncEmitsEachScope`).

**Fix 2:** Added an explanatory comment in `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go` at the `entry.Summary = &s` site in `renderHierarchyJSON`, documenting that per-scope summaries are intentionally embedded raw via `json.Marshal(doc)` rather than `report.MarshalJSON` — the aggregate carries its own top-level `schema_version`, embedded summaries match the standalone shape minus the version wrapper, and `targets` is populated by `Summarize`. Verified against `internal/report/json.go` and `summary.go` that the claim is accurate. Output shape unchanged (comment-only).

**Gate (all green):**
- `go test -race ./internal/cli/...` → `ok ... 5.243s`
- `go vet ./...` → clean; `golangci-lint run` → `0 issues.`
- `go build ./...` → clean

**Commit SHA:** `9b503257a0436d5a295e2734a11ea4213430efd9` on `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests` (2 files changed, 85 insertions).
Plan 2 complete and verified (continue-and-report now tested end-to-end, gate green). Moving to Plan 3.

You are implementing the final feature slice in the agent-sync Go project at /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, on branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests` (already checked out — do NOT create/switch branches).

## Your task
Execute the plan at `docs/plans/2026-06-17-003-feat-coverage-and-status-plan.md` (Tasks 1–4) in order. Read it fully first. Task 1 (the `internal/coverage` package) contains exact code — implement it verbatim (adjust only if it genuinely fails to compile, and report any change). Tasks 2–3 are integration work against existing code.

## Read before integrating (Tasks 2–3)
- `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go` and `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go` (from Plan 2) — the orchestrator `runHierarchySync`, `scopeOutcome`, `renderHierarchyText`, `renderHierarchyJSON`/`hierarchyScopeJSON`, the `resolveHome` test seam, and the `hierarchyTree`/`newTestRuntime` test helpers. REUSE these; do not invent new harnesses.
- `internal/cli/cmd_status.go` — current single-scope status. Find and read its test file (likely `internal/cli/cmd_statusvalidate_test.go`) before changing it.
- `internal/hierarchy` (built): `Discover(cwd, Options{Home, IncludeUser, MaxHops}) ([]Scope, error)`, `Scope{Root, ManifestPath, Level, Emit}`, `Level.String()`. The user scope is returned with `Emit=false` and must be LISTED by status (read-only).
- `internal/ledger` `Load(root, target)`, `internal/manifest` `LoadFile`, `internal/fsroot` `OpenWorkspaceRoot`.

## Hard requirements
- `internal/coverage` imports only `ir` + `hierarchy` (NO `adapter` import — targets are plain name strings). Unknown targets/kinds must default to native (no false warnings) — there is a test for this; keep it green.
- Coverage warnings surface in `sync` output (per scope, computed from the decoded IR's distinct kinds + the scope's manifest targets + the scope level). The project/user levels never warn; only `directory` level can.
- `status` becomes hierarchy-aware: list ALL discovered scopes (user read-only + project + directory) with level/root/source/per-target managed counts loaded from each scope's own ledger. Status performs NO staging/swap and calls NO engine; opening an fsroot only to READ ledgers is allowed. When `--workspace` is set, keep today's single-scope status. Continue-and-report if one scope's manifest fails to load (don't abort the whole status).
- Do NOT modify `internal/engine`.

## Verification gate (must be green before reporting DONE)
- `go test -race -cover ./internal/coverage/... ./internal/cli/... ./internal/hierarchy/...`
- `go vet ./... && golangci-lint run`
- `go build ./... && go test ./...`
Fix anything surfaced (errcheck/govet/staticcheck/unused/gosimple/ineffassign/unparam are enabled). Make the per-task commits with the plan's exact messages.

## If existing status tests break
The status output shape legitimately changes (now multi-scope). Update existing status tests to match the new shape ONLY where the change is legitimate, preserving their original intent (don't weaken assertions to force a pass). Report exactly which tests you changed and why.

## If blocked
Adapt intent-level integration code to the real code and report deviations. If you hit genuine architectural ambiguity, STOP and report BLOCKED with specifics rather than guessing.

## Report back
Status (DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT), final gate output (race/cover, vet, lint, build, full `go test ./...`), commits (SHAs + messages), files changed, any existing tests you modified (with why), deviations from the plan, and concerns.

content.text:
Tree is clean, all commits landed.

## Status: DONE

All four tasks executed in order. Task 1's `internal/coverage` package was implemented verbatim from the plan — it compiled and passed with no changes required.

### Final gate output (all green)
- **`go test -race -cover ./internal/coverage/... ./internal/cli/... ./internal/hierarchy/...`**: PASS — coverage 100.0%, cli 70.4%, hierarchy 88.7%
- **`go vet ./...`**: clean
- **`golangci-lint run`**: `0 issues.`
- **`go build ./...`**: clean
- **`go test ./...`**: all 33 packages PASS
- **Manual smoke**: confirmed end-to-end. `sync` prints the coverage warning on the nested (directory) scope only, project scope clean. `status` lists user (read-only/untracked) + project + directory scopes with level/root/source/per-target counts.

### Commits
- `8d1a126` feat(coverage): native-support analyzer for hierarchy levels
- `7532423` feat(cli): surface coverage warnings in hierarchy sync output
- `a562ec7` feat(cli): hierarchy-aware status across all discovered scopes

(Task 4's optional cleanup commit was skipped per the plan — no lint/race fixes were needed.)

### Files changed
- **Created**: `internal/coverage/coverage.go`, `internal/coverage/coverage_test.go`
- **Modified**: `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync.go`, `internal/cli/hierarchy_sync_test.go`, `internal/cli/cmd_status.go`, `internal/cli/cmd_statusvalidate_test.go`

### Existing tests modified
None. The existing status tests (`TestStatus_ReportsSourceAndTargets`, `TestStatus_WatchFailedBanner`) all drive status via `--workspace` (the `runCmd` helper), which keeps the single-scope path I preserved unchanged — so they passed without edits. I only **added** new tests (`TestStatusShowsHierarchy`, `TestStatusHierarchyJSONListsScopes`, `TestHierarchySyncEmitsCoverageWarning`).

### Deviations from the plan
1. **`kindsOf` placed in `hierarchy_sync.go`** (not a separate file) and computed right after a successful `prepareScope` (before `engine.Sync`), exactly as the plan's preferred "simplest" option.
2. **Status JSON shape**: the plan said "keep existing top-level fields for the single-scope path or when only one scope exists." I implemented this as two distinct documents — the single-scope path (`--workspace`) emits the unchanged `statusReport` (with top-level `workspace`/`source`/`commit`/`targets`), and the hierarchy path emits a `{"scopes":[...]}` document. The single-scope path's JSON/text output is byte-identical to before, so no consumer of the `--workspace` path breaks.

### Concerns
- **Watch-failed banner not surfaced on the hierarchy status path.** The `last-watch.failed` marker is per-workspace-root, and the multi-scope view has no single root to check. The marker is still surfaced on the single-scope (`--workspace`) path, so `TestStatus_WatchFailedBanner` passes. If you want the banner in hierarchy mode, the natural spot is per-scope (check the marker under each scope's root) — flagging for a follow-up, not implemented here since the plan didn't call for it.
- The directory-level native-support table (`claude`/`codex` nested prose, `cursor` nested rules) is the project's documented assumption per the plan's design-decision §3; correcting an entry is a one-line table edit if a tool's behavior is verified to differ.

Skeptical spec-compliance review. Verify by reading actual code, not the report. Repo: /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/agent-sync, branch `feat/hierarchy-aware-manifests`.

## Spec
`docs/plans/2026-06-17-003-feat-coverage-and-status-plan.md` (coverage analyzer + hierarchy status). Note the plan's explicit "Design decisions locked" section (coverage in its own package; native table keyed by target name; coverage surfaces in sync not status; directory-level native rules for claude/codex/cursor).

## Diff
`git diff d9686bf..a562ec7` — `internal/coverage/` (new) and `internal/cli/` (hierarchy_sync.go, hierarchy_sync_test.go, cmd_status.go, cmd_statusvalidate_test.go).

## Verify independently
1. **coverage package**: `Analyze(level, kinds, targets)` returns nil for project/user levels; for directory level, warns for kinds NOT in the per-target native set; unknown targets default to native (no warnings); deterministic ordering. Confirm the native table matches the plan's documented rule: claude→agents-md native; codex→agents-md native; cursor→rule native; everything else at directory level warns. Confirm `internal/coverage` imports ONLY `ir` + `hierarchy` (no `adapter`) — run `go list -deps` or read imports.
2. **sync wiring**: `scopeOutcome` carries `Warnings []coverage.Warning`; computed from the scope's decoded IR kinds + manifest targets + scope level; rendered in text (indented under scope) and JSON (`coverage_warnings`). Verify the new test asserts a directory-level claude skill warns while the project scope does not.
3. **status hierarchy**: default path uses `hierarchy.Discover`, lists ALL scopes incl the read-only user scope (Emit=false), shows level/root/source/per-target ledger counts; `--workspace` keeps single-scope; status calls NO engine and does no staging. Confirm continue-and-report if a scope's manifest fails to load.
4. **engine untouched**: `git diff d9686bf..a562ec7 -- internal/engine/` is empty.
5. **No weakened existing tests**: check the diff of `internal/cli/cmd_statusvalidate_test.go` — were existing assertions preserved (only additions), or were any existing tests loosened to pass? Report specifically.
6. **Tests real**: assert on-disk/output behavior; run `go test -race ./internal/coverage/... ./internal/cli/...` and confirm pass.
7. **Missing/extra** vs the plan.

## Report
✅ Spec compliant OR ❌ Issues found (file:line, specific). Call out any deviation and whether the plan documented/permitted it.

content.text:
## ✅ Spec compliant

Verified by reading the actual code at `d9686bf..a562ec7`, not the report. All seven plan claims hold.

**1. Coverage package** (`internal/coverage/coverage.go`) — `Analyze(level, kinds, targets)` returns `nil` for non-directory levels (line 63, covers project/user). Directory level warns for kinds not in the per-target native set. Native table (lines 45-49) matches the plan exactly: `claude→agents-md`, `codex→agents-md`, `cursor→rule`. Unknown targets skipped via `known()` (lines 67-69) → default native, no warnings. Deterministic sort by target then kind (lines 84-89). `go list -deps` confirms the only internal imports are `hierarchy` and `ir` (plus their transitive `workspace/cache/git`) — no `adapter`, no cycle.

**2. Sync wiring** (`hierarchy_sync.go`) — `scopeOutcome.Warnings []coverage.Warning` (line 28); computed from `coverage.Analyze(sc.Level, kindsOf(req.Nodes), req.Targets)` after a successful prepare (line 72), independent of sync outcome. `kindsOf` dedups (lines 87-98). Text render indents `  warning: <Detail>` under the scope header (lines 135-138); JSON uses `coverage_warnings,omitempty` (line 151). `TestHierarchySyncEmitsCoverageWarning` asserts the directory-level claude skill yields exactly 1 warning (claude/skill/directory) while the project scope yields 0, and that the warning surfaces in text output.

**3. Status hierarchy** (`cmd_status.go`) — when `Workspace == ""` uses `hierarchy.Discover(cwd, {Home: home})` (line 143, no IncludeUser), lists all scopes including the user scope (`ReadOnly: !sc.Emit`, line 154); records level/root/source/per-target ledger counts. `--workspace` keeps the original single-scope `statusReport` path (lines 82-122). No engine call, no staging — only `manifest.LoadFile` + read-only `fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot` to read ledgers. Continue-and-report on manifest load failure (lines 156-162). Confirmed via `internal/hierarchy/discover.go` that the user scope is returned whenever home holds a manifest, with `Emit=includeUser`.

**4. Engine untouched** — `git diff d9686bf..a562ec7 -- internal/engine/` is empty.

**5. No weakened existing tests** — the `cmd_statusvalidate_test.go` diff is purely additive (two new tests + one helper inserted between existing tests). `TestStatus_ReportsSourceAndTargets` is byte-for-byte unchanged and still asserts source path + `untracked`/`managed file`; it routes through `runCmd` which injects `--workspace` (line 16), so it still exercises the single-scope path. No assertion was loosened.

**6. Tests real** — assertions check on-disk emission (`mustExist`/`mustNotExist`), output substrings, and JSON structure (3 scopes, first is user/read-only). `go test -race ./internal/coverage/... ./internal/cli/...` passes; `go vet` + `go build ./...` clean.

**7. Missing/extra vs plan** — No gaps. One extra test beyond the plan (`TestStatusHierarchyJSONListsScopes`), which strengthens coverage of the JSON `scopes` array. The plan's three documented deviations (coverage in its own package; static name-keyed table; warnings in sync not status) are all implemented as described and remain consistent with the design.

No issues found.
